panel.origin: Radio shorthand meaning 'message received and understood.' One of the most recognizable bits of military and aviation communication.
panel.mixology: 'Proceed to checkpoint Bravo.' 'Roger,' the pilot replied.
panel.examples: From the older phonetic alphabet, where 'Roger' stood for the letter 'R' — for 'received'. The word stuck even after the alphabet changed to 'Romeo'.

panel.origin: 'Copy' means a message was received; 'Wilco' (will comply) means received and the order will be carried out. A subtle but important distinction.
panel.mixology: 'Copy that,' she said, noting the coordinates. 'Wilco,' he added, already moving to comply.
panel.examples: 'Copy' from receiving/copying down a transmission; 'Wilco' is a contraction of 'will comply'. 'Roger Wilco' redundantly means 'understood, and I'll do it'.

panel.origin: 'Over' means 'I'm done speaking, your turn'; 'Out' means 'this conversation is finished.' Contrary to movies, 'over and out' is a contradiction.
panel.mixology: 'Returning to base, over.' ... 'Understood. Out.'
panel.examples: Precise radio procedure: 'over' invites a reply, 'out' ends the exchange. Saying 'over and out' — common in film — is technically nonsensical, mixing the two.

panel.origin: 'Affirmative' means yes; 'negative' means no. Used instead of the easily-misheard 'yeah' or 'nah' over crackly radio.
panel.mixology: 'Do you have visual?' 'Affirmative.' 'Any casualties?' 'Negative.'
panel.examples: From 'affirm' and 'negate'. The full, distinct words resist being garbled or misheard over noisy radio far better than a quick 'yes' or 'no'.

panel.origin: The international distress call signaling a life-threatening emergency, repeated three times. The most serious call a pilot or sailor can make.
panel.mixology: 'Mayday, mayday, mayday,' the pilot transmitted as both engines failed.
panel.examples: From the French 'm'aidez' / 'venez m'aider' (help me / come help me), chosen in the 1920s because it sounded clear and distinct over radio to English and French ears.

panel.origin: The phonetic alphabet (Alpha, Bravo, Charlie...) spells out letters with distinct words so they can't be confused over radio. Essential for clear comms.
panel.mixology: He spelled the callsign 'Foxtrot Golf November' so there could be no mistaking it.
panel.examples: The NATO phonetic alphabet, standardized in the 1950s, assigns an unmistakable word to each letter — vital when a misheard letter could cost lives.

panel.origin: The proper way to ask for a message to be repeated. Pilots and soldiers never say 'repeat' on the radio — that word means something else entirely.
panel.mixology: 'You're breaking up — say again your last,' she radioed.
panel.examples: 'Repeat' is avoided because, in artillery contexts, it specifically means 'fire again'. 'Say again' safely requests a repeat without risking a deadly misunderstanding.

panel.origin: An instruction to set a transponder code, which identifies an aircraft on radar. 'Squawk 7700' signals an emergency to air traffic control.
panel.mixology: Air traffic control instructed the pilot to squawk a specific code for identification.
panel.examples: From a WWII transponder system codenamed 'Parrot' — hence the bird-like 'squawk' for the electronic signal an aircraft emits to identify itself on radar.

panel.origin: An aircraft's height above a reference point, usually sea level or the ground. The vertical dimension pilots constantly monitor and manage.
panel.mixology: The pilot climbed to a cruising altitude of 35,000 feet.
panel.examples: From Latin 'altitudo' (height), from 'altus' (high). One of the fundamental measurements of flight, distinct from altitude above ground versus above sea level.

panel.origin: The compass direction an aircraft is pointed, measured in degrees from 0 to 360. 'Heading 270' means flying due west.
panel.mixology: Air traffic control instructed her to turn to a heading of 090, due east.
panel.examples: From 'head' — the direction the nose 'heads' toward. Expressed in degrees so pilots and controllers share a precise, unambiguous sense of direction.

panel.origin: The speed of an aircraft relative to the air it moves through — which differs from its speed over the ground, especially in strong winds. Critical for safe flight.
panel.mixology: A strong headwind meant her airspeed was high while her speed over the ground crawled.
panel.examples: A compound of 'air' + 'speed'. The distinction from ground speed matters enormously — a wing's lift depends on airspeed, not how fast the ground passes below.

panel.origin: An aircraft's orientation relative to the horizon — its pitch (nose up/down) and roll (banking). Not to be confused with altitude (height).
panel.mixology: He corrected the aircraft's attitude, leveling the wings and lowering the nose.
panel.examples: From the same root as 'attitude' (a posture or disposition) — here, the plane's physical posture in the air. A classic source of confusion with the similar-sounding 'altitude'.

panel.origin: A dangerous loss of lift when a wing exceeds its critical angle and airflow separates — not an engine failure, but an aerodynamic one. The plane stops flying.
panel.mixology: Pulling the nose up too steeply, the trainee induced a stall and the aircraft began to drop.
panel.examples: From 'stall' (to stop or stick). Crucially, an aerodynamic stall is about the wing losing lift, not the engine quitting — a frequent public misconception.

panel.origin: Instrument Landing System — radio guidance that helps pilots land precisely in poor visibility, providing both horizontal and vertical alignment to the runway.
panel.mixology: In thick fog, she flew the ILS approach, following the beams down to the runway.
panel.examples: An acronym for the 'instrument landing system'. By following its radio beams, pilots can align with and descend to a runway they may not even be able to see.

panel.origin: Flight Level — a standardized altitude expressed in hundreds of feet at a common pressure setting, used at higher altitudes. 'Flight Level 350' is roughly 35,000 feet.
panel.mixology: The jet leveled off at Flight Level 390 for the long cruise.
panel.examples: Above a transition altitude, planes use 'flight levels' on a standard pressure setting, so all aircraft share the same reference and stay safely separated vertically.

panel.origin: Aborting a landing attempt to climb back up and try again, when the approach isn't safe. A normal, trained-for maneuver, not a failure.
panel.mixology: With another aircraft still on the runway, the pilot executed a go-around and circled back.
panel.examples: Descriptive: the aircraft 'goes around' for another attempt. A routine safety maneuver — far better to abort and retry than force an unsafe landing.

panel.origin: Reconnaissance — gathering information about an area, enemy, or situation, often before an operation. Scouting to reduce the unknowns.
panel.mixology: The team ran recon on the compound for two days before the mission.
panel.examples: A clipping of 'reconnaissance', from French 'reconnaître' (to recognize, explore). The vital practice of scouting ahead to learn before acting.

panel.origin: Situation Report — a concise update on the current state of affairs: position, status, threats, and needs. Keeps everyone informed.
panel.mixology: 'Give me a sitrep,' the commander ordered, wanting the latest on the ground.
panel.examples: A compound abbreviation of 'situation report'. One of many '-rep' military shorthands, it delivers a fast, structured snapshot of current conditions.

panel.origin: ROE — the directives defining when, where, and how force may be used. They govern what soldiers are and aren't permitted to do in a given operation.
panel.mixology: Strict rules of engagement meant they couldn't fire unless fired upon first.
panel.examples: Descriptive: the 'rules' governing 'engagement' (combat). ROE translate law, policy, and strategy into concrete limits on the use of force.

panel.origin: 'Cover' protects from enemy fire (a solid wall); 'concealment' only hides you from view (a bush). Confusing the two can be fatal.
panel.mixology: He ducked behind the concrete barrier for cover, not just the flimsy bushes that offered mere concealment.
panel.examples: A critical distinction: 'cover' physically stops bullets, while 'concealment' only blocks line of sight. A soldier must always know which one they have.

panel.origin: The side of a military formation, or to attack from the side. 'Flanking' an enemy means hitting them from a vulnerable angle rather than head-on.
panel.mixology: While one squad held the front, the other moved to flank the enemy from the right.
panel.examples: From the anatomical 'flank' (the side of the body). Attacking the exposed side of an enemy force is one of the oldest and most effective tactics in warfare.

panel.origin: 'Exfil' (exfiltration) is the planned withdrawal from an operational area; 'infil' (infiltration) is moving in. Getting out is often the hardest part.
panel.mixology: After securing the intel, they moved to the exfil point for extraction.
panel.examples: Clippings of 'exfiltration' and 'infiltration'. The planned routes and methods for slipping into and back out of an objective area, often the riskiest phases.

panel.origin: A warning that friendly forces are dangerously near the target of an air strike or artillery fire, requiring extra caution to avoid hitting one's own troops.
panel.mixology: He called in the strike 'danger close,' the enemy nearly on top of his own position.
panel.examples: Descriptive: friendlies are 'dangerously close' to the impact zone. The call signals that supporting fire risks harming the very troops it's meant to protect.

panel.origin: Non-Commissioned Officer — an enlisted leader (like a sergeant) who rose through the ranks, rather than being commissioned as an officer. The 'backbone' of the military.
panel.mixology: The young lieutenant leaned heavily on his experienced NCO's hard-won wisdom.
panel.examples: An acronym for 'non-commissioned officer'. NCOs lead from experience rather than commission, and are often called the backbone that holds a unit together.

panel.origin: 'CO' is the Commanding Officer (in charge); 'XO' is the Executive Officer (second-in-command, handling day-to-day operations). The top of a unit's leadership.
panel.mixology: The CO set the mission's intent while the XO handled the detailed planning.
panel.examples: Acronyms for 'commanding officer' and 'executive officer'. The CO holds ultimate authority; the XO is the deputy who runs the organization's daily machinery.

panel.origin: Zulu Time — Coordinated Universal Time (UTC), used as a single global time reference so forces in different zones can coordinate without confusion.
panel.mixology: The operation was set for 0600 Zulu, the same instant everywhere regardless of local time.
panel.examples: 'Zulu' is the phonetic letter 'Z', the designator for the UTC time zone. Using one universal clock prevents deadly errors when units span the globe.

panel.origin: Military time uses a 24-hour clock spoken in a distinct way — '1300 hours' for 1 p.m., '0600' as 'zero six hundred.' It eliminates a.m./p.m. confusion.
panel.mixology: 'We move at 2200 hours,' she said — ten o'clock at night, no ambiguity.
panel.examples: The 24-hour clock spoken military-style. Saying 'oh-six-hundred' removes any chance of confusing morning and evening, critical when timing must be exact.

panel.origin: The hierarchy of authority through which orders pass, from top to bottom. Respecting the chain of command keeps a large organization coordinated.
panel.mixology: He took his concern up the chain of command rather than going straight to the general.
panel.examples: Descriptive: the 'chain' of linked authority running through the 'command' structure. Each link reports to the one above, keeping orders and accountability orderly.

panel.origin: After-Action Review — a structured debrief held after an operation to analyze what happened, what worked, what didn't, and how to improve. Learning from every mission.
panel.mixology: In the AAR, the team honestly dissected every mistake to do better next time.
panel.examples: An acronym for 'after-action review'. This disciplined, blame-light analysis of an event became so effective it's now widely borrowed by businesses and other fields.

panel.origin: Radio slang for 'On the Move' (the phonetic letters O and M). 'We're Oscar Mike' means a unit is underway.
panel.mixology: 'Oscar Mike,' he radioed as the convoy rolled out.
panel.examples: From the phonetic alphabet: 'Oscar' (O) and 'Mike' (M) spell 'OM', short for 'on the move'. A crisp way to report that a unit is now underway.

panel.origin: 'FUBAR' and 'SNAFU' are colorful acronyms describing things gone badly wrong — 'SNAFU' meaning a situation that's a mess, 'FUBAR' meaning thoroughly broken.
panel.mixology: The plan went completely FUBAR the moment the weather turned.
panel.examples: WWII-era acronyms (politely, 'Fouled Up Beyond All Recognition' and 'Situation Normal: All Fouled Up'). Wry soldier humor for the chaos and mishaps of military life.

panel.origin: An all-purpose Army exclamation expressing enthusiasm, agreement, or motivation — meaning roughly 'yes,' 'understood,' or 'hell yeah.' (The Marines say 'Oorah.')
panel.mixology: 'Are we ready to move?' 'Hooah!' the squad roared back.
panel.examples: Of debated origin, possibly from 'HUA' (Heard, Understood, Acknowledged). This versatile battle cry conveys spirit and assent — the Marine equivalent is 'Oorah'.

panel.origin: Military slang for a kilometer. 'The objective is two klicks north' means two kilometers away. Standard distance shorthand in the field.
panel.mixology: 'Target's about three klicks east,' the scout reported.
panel.examples: Slang for 'kilometer', of uncertain origin — possibly from the clicking adjustment of a rifle sight or map tools. The standard field term for a kilometer of distance.

panel.origin: 'Got your six' means 'I've got your back' — your 'six' is the position directly behind you, like six o'clock on a clock face. A pledge of protection.
panel.mixology: 'Don't worry, I've got your six,' she said, covering his rear as he advanced.
panel.examples: From the clock-position system pilots use, where 12 is ahead and 6 is directly behind. 'Watch your six' warns of a threat to your rear; 'got your six' promises cover.

panel.origin: 'POG' (Person Other than Grunt) is slang for non-combat support troops; a 'grunt' is an infantry soldier. The terms mark a good-natured (and sometimes pointed) rivalry.
panel.mixology: The grunts ribbed the POGs about their cushy desks far from the front lines.
panel.examples: 'Grunt' evokes the infantryman's hard, grunting labor; 'POG' (person other than grunt) covers everyone else. The banter reflects an age-old combat-vs-support divide.
